result.local_summary: The supplied chunk is the opening of a LaTeX article titled "RLM-FORGE: Runtime-Lifted Recursive Language Models for Agent Infrastructure" by JQ Lee, dated May 4, 2026. It defines document metadata and packages, then presents an abstract and begins the Introduction. The abstract argues that Recursive Language Models can be runtime-lifted into an existing agent runtime rather than requiring a new recursive model checkpoint. RLM-FORGE is described as implementing this by mapping RLM sub-calls onto Hermes Agent RPC, while Ouroboros owns recursive state, scheduling, trace replay, and evidence validation. The paper introduces TraceGuard as a deterministic evidence gate for parent synthesis, reports live truncation and portability results, distinguishes guarded runtime memory from admissible factual evidence, and frames the core claim as a systems/runtime claim rather than a benchmark-winning claim.
key_claims_in_chunk: RLM-FORGE treats RLM as a deployable runtime primitive for recursive inference, not as a new model architecture.
The runtime primitive preserves bounded context partitioning, explicit sub-call graphs, replayable intermediate evidence, and rejectable parent synthesis.
TraceGuard is introduced as a deterministic evidence gate for parent synthesis.
The live Hermes truncation fixture shows parity between vanilla and recursive execution after scorer correction.
A 24-cell live portability run across Hermes+GLM, Claude Code, and Codex completes with 24 mandatory contract passes.
The same primary matrix passes with experimental read-write operational memory enabled.
Memory is limited to schema and retry priors and is not admitted as factual evidence.
A layered-memory effect is observed because the Hermes adapter does not pass --ignore-rules, allowing Hermes MEMORY.md to be injected before the explicit RLM-FORGE envelope.

Deterministic memory-runtime benchmarks isolate a narrow benefit: guarded memory rejects answer-memory contamination, separates Hermes prompt memory from RLM-FORGE guarded memory, and reduces repeated repair calls under generated schema-failure profiles.
The strongest supported statement is bounded: memory can improve runtime control and repair efficiency without becoming admissible factual evidence.

Lines 77-80 begin the Introduction and cite Recursive Language Models introduced by Zhang et al. 2025.

result.atomic_summary: This chunk frames RLM-FORGE as a runtime-first implementation of Recursive Language Model behavior within an existing agent stack. It argues that RLM should be understood not only as a model-training or inference paradigm for long-context reasoning, but also as a runtime interface: bounded subcalls can process context slices, return structured evidence, and be orchestrated recursively by an outer controller even without a natively recursive model architecture. The chunk distinguishes quality advantage from control advantage, emphasizing that runtime recursion makes context partitionable, work compositional, intermediate claims inspectable, and parent synthesis evidence-gated. It then states the paper's concrete systems claims: Hermes provides the callable inner execution layer, Ouroboros provides recursion control, trace storage, and acceptance-criteria decomposition, and TraceGuard enforces evidence-gated synthesis. The claims include runtime-lifted RLM through Hermes RPC, preservation of RLM control surfaces, incremental integration through scaffold and AC-decomposition paths, executable evidence discipline with deterministic and live matrix results, memory without evidence leakage, and evidence-admissible memory as runtime policy. The chunk closes by limiting the paper's novelty claim: it does not claim novelty over RLM itself or compare against the post-trained RLM-Qwen3-8B model, but instead claims that RLM can be hosted as a runtime primitive by existing agent infrastructure.

key_claims.claim: RLM can be interpreted as a runtime interface, not only as a long-context reasoning paradigm.
key_claims.evidence: Lines 81-87 state that an LLM can be invoked on bounded context slices, return structured evidence, and be called again under outer control, allowing the runtime to supply the recursive substrate.

key_claims.claim: RLM-FORGE integrates RLM into an existing agent runtime without requiring new model architecture, sandbox, or transport layer.
key_claims.evidence: Lines 88-96 ask whether RLM advantages can move into an existing runtime and identify Hermes, Ouroboros, and TraceGuard as the concrete stack.

key_claims.claim: The paper distinguishes RLM quality advantages from runtime control advantages.
key_claims.evidence: Lines 98-107 explain that even if a single vanilla LLM call can be correct, runtime recursion changes inference by partitioning context, composing work, inspecting intermediate claims, and blocking unsupported parent synthesis.

key_claims.claim: Hermes maps onto the RLM sub-call envelope through an RPC pattern.
key_claims.evidence: Lines 111-116 describe Hermes scripts calling tools through RPC to collapse multi-step pipelines into zero-context turns, making RLM executable as an agent-runtime capability.

key_claims.claim: RLM-FORGE preserves core RLM control surfaces at runtime.

key_claims.claim: TraceGuard enforces executable evidence discipline by rejecting unsupported structured claims.
key_claims.evidence: Lines 131-143 state that TraceGuard rejects claims lacking accepted child evidence handles and summarize deterministic contract ablations and live primary matrix results.

key_claims.claim: The harness includes a memory mode that avoids evidence leakage by rebuilding accepted evidence from fresh child outputs.
key_claims.evidence: Lines 145-152 state that memory stores only allowlisted operational observations and that the accepted evidence manifest is rebuilt from fresh child outputs, not durable memory.

key_claims.claim: Hermes built-in memory and RLM-FORGE guarded memory have separate runtime roles.

key_claims.claim: The paper's novelty claim is systems-level, not a claim of novelty over RLM itself or a comparison to RLM-Qwen3-8B.

result.atomic_summary: This chunk establishes related work and system design context for RLM-FORGE. It defines Recursive Language Models as an inference strategy where an outer LLM call invokes itself or another LLM on a transformed input slice through an RLM tool call, with the original RLM paper using a Python REPL environment exposing peek, partition, transform, and recursive call operations. It then characterizes Hermes Agent as a provider-agnostic, open-source self-improving AI agent runtime whose CLI can swap the inner LLM across providers and whose RPC tool mechanism and isolated subagent support make it suitable as a structured recursive boundary. The chunk also identifies Ouroboros as contributing an unchanged typed acceptance-criteria tree with depth cap five and a HermesCliRuntime adapter wrapping Hermes CLI as an AgentRuntime. The System Design section introduces runtime lifting: converting the RLM inference pattern into a stable runtime contract where an outer scaffold selects bounded context, invokes an inner LM, records evidence, and chooses whether to recurse, summarize, synthesize, retry, or terminate. It contrasts this with map-reduce by emphasizing a validated call graph with parent-child relationships, bounded inputs, typed modes, evidence handles, and accepted or rejected child results. The included figure depicts Ouroboros controlling recursive scheduling and trace state, Hermes executing bounded sub-calls, and TraceGuard validating parent synthesis against accepted child evidence before either rejecting unsupported synthesis or committing accepted synthesis.

key_points.claim: RLM is presented as an inference strategy where an outer LLM invokes itself or another LLM on a transformed slice of input using an RLM tool call.
The original RLM environment is described as a Python REPL holding input context and exposing peek, partition, transform, and recursive call operations.

The cited headline RLM result is processing inputs up to two orders of magnitude beyond model context windows while often outperforming vanilla calls and common long-context scaffolds across four diverse tasks.
Hermes Agent is described as an open-source self-improving AI agent runtime by Nous Research with provider-agnostic model swapping via the hermes model CLI subcommand.
Hermes RPC tools and isolated subagent spawning are positioned as yielding a runtime callable like a structured JSON function, fitting the recursive boundary needed by an RLM scaffold.
Ouroboros contributes a typed acceptance-criteria tree with depth cap five and a HermesCliRuntime adapter wrapping the Hermes CLI as an AgentRuntime, both used unchanged.
Runtime lifting is defined as exposing a model-level or research-environment inference pattern as a stable runtime contract executable by ordinary agent infrastructure.
For RLM, the runtime contract consists of bounded-context selection, inner-LM invocation, evidence recording, and a decision to recurse, summarize, synthesize, retry, or terminate.
Runtime-lifted RLM is distinguished from map-reduce because it produces a call graph with parent, bounded input, typed mode, evidence handles, and accepted or rejected results.
The parent synthesis step is described as runtime-validated rather than a free-form final answer.
The figure depicts an Ouroboros controller, Hermes bounded sub-calls, and a TraceGuard parent-synthesis gate that validates structured claims against accepted child evidence.

result.parent_synthesis_utility: This chunk can support parent-level synthesis about the paper's related-work framing, the motivation for using Hermes as the recursive runtime boundary, the specific unchanged Ouroboros components reused, and the central design claim that RLM-FORGE implements runtime-lifted RLM as a traceable, evidence-gated call graph rather than ordinary map-reduce.

result.atomic_summary: This chunk presents the runtime architecture and control split for RLM-FORGE. It states that Ouroboros owns recursive orchestration, including scheduling, termination, guardrails, and trace persistence, while Hermes acts only as a bounded inner language model invoked through the HermesCliRuntime adapter. The chunk emphasizes that Hermes must not recursively call Ouroboros or invoke ooo commands inside the RLM loop, and that this restriction is enforced both by prompt-level instructions and a structured response contract. It identifies TraceGuard as the validator that determines whether parent synthesis may commit claims based on child observations. The chunk also defines the outer-to-inner handoff envelope fields: mode, call_context, constraints, context, and evidence_handles. It defines the expected response fields as verdict, confidence, result, evidence references, and residual gaps, all of which are validated before state mutation. A table summarizes RLM-FORGE runtime advantages: bounded sub-calls over selected chunks for context beyond one prompt, explicit parent-child call graphs for compositional reasoning, child evidence handles and replayable outputs for inspectability, TraceGuard validation for safer synthesis, and HermesCliRuntime for provider portability. The chunk begins the subsection on sub-call lifecycle but only includes the setup line introducing the lifecycle enumeration.

key_claims.claim: RLM-FORGE separates orchestration and inference: Ouroboros controls recursive state while Hermes performs bounded local inference.
Hermes is explicitly prohibited from recursively invoking Ouroboros or running ooo commands during the RLM loop.
TraceGuard validates whether parent synthesis may commit claims based on child-observed evidence.
The handoff from Ouroboros to Hermes is a structured envelope containing mode, call_context, constraints, context, and evidence_handles.
Hermes responses are structured with verdict, confidence, result, evidence references, and residual gaps, and are validated before outer state mutation.

The paper’s stated main thesis is that RLM-FORGE preserves runtime RLM advantages through concrete control surfaces in a real agent runtime, even though the live fixture does not show a quality delta.

result.local_summary: This chunk describes the RLM runtime boundary and experiment setup. It lists host-side duties around Hermes sub-call execution: validating guardrails, binding bounded context, recording trace events, invoking Hermes through HermesCliRuntime, validating the returned JSON envelope, committing only accepted results into recursive state, and scheduling follow-up work. It states that Hermes may recommend atomic, decomposition, retry, or synthesis readiness, but only Ouroboros controls recursive flow. The chunk then identifies two integration paths: a dedicated RLMOuterScaffoldLoop used by the user-facing `ooo rlm` command, and an optional Hermes-backed extension to `decompose_ac` that leaves `ooo run` and `ooo evolve` on the original default path because they do not pass `hermes_runtime`. Finally, it frames the experiment as a runtime/control-surface demonstration rather than a leaderboard-quality comparison, describes a deterministic long-context truncation fixture, and notes that HermesCliRuntime v0.11.0 is used against a private OpenAI-compatible endpoint with public reproduction possible through any Hermes-supported provider.

salient_claims.claim: The host validates guardrails, binds bounded context, records trace events, invokes Hermes, validates JSON envelopes, commits accepted results, records invalid responses as failures, and schedules follow-up control actions.
Hermes can recommend atomicity, decomposition, retryability, or synthesis readiness, but Ouroboros alone converts those recommendations into recursive control flow.
The first integration path is a dedicated RLMOuterScaffoldLoop that owns scheduling, AC-tree mutations, sub-call dispatch, parent synthesis, and termination, and is exercised by `ooo rlm`.
The second integration path extends `decompose_ac` with an optional `hermes_runtime`; when absent, the original LLM-only flow remains in use.
`ooo run` and `ooo evolve` do not pass `hermes_runtime`, so they continue through the tested default path.

The experiment tests whether the Hermes-backed stack can execute vanilla and recursive RLM call graphs, persist artifacts, replay recursive traces, and score omitted-fact safety; the reported live-fixture quality result is a tie.
The long-context fixture has twelve lines split across six two-line chunks, with four retained facts and two omitted fact IDs beyond the truncation boundary.
The fixture comparison is controlled because both vanilla and recursive runs receive the same selected-omitted partition and are scored by the same quality code.
Hermes is invoked through HermesCliRuntime v0.11.0 against a custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint; model and provider are configured externally.

result.summary: This chunk documents the live truncation artifact environment, then presents a multi-runtime portability harness for the primary RLM-FORGE+TraceGuard contract. The environment table records Hermes CLI v0.11.0, a custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint, a provider-anonymized frontier-tier inner model, Ouroboros dependency commit 5b4bc10e, the command ooo rlm --truncation-benchmark, fixture rlm-long-context-truncation-v1, sub-call counts vanilla=1 and recursive=5, and notes that latency, token count, and monetary cost were not persisted. The recursive RLM configuration is max_depth=5 and ambiguity_threshold=0.2, while the truncation fixture required only depth two in practice. The portability section states that an 8-fixture, 3-family, 4-contract matrix plan contains 96 cells, but this version executes only the primary RLM-FORGE+TraceGuard contract across 8 fixtures and 3 runtime families, producing 24 live cells. Each completed cell uses four child calls plus one parent synthesis call through the same AgentRuntime surface, with identical fixtures, prompts, schemas, scoring, and TraceGuard logic; only runtime adapter and model alias vary. The primary portability table reports 8/8 pass for hermes_glm via HermesCliRuntime with glm-4.7, 8/8 pass for claude_code_opus47 via ClaudeAgentAdapter with opus, and 8/8 pass for codex_gpt55 via CodexCliRuntime with gpt-5.5. The text concludes that all 24 live cells completed with mandatory contract passes and aggregate pass status. It also explains that a chunk-only citation trap remains useful because parent claims must carry both fact_id and evidence_chunk_id. An earlier Hermes+GLM failure omitted the evidence handle, causing a missing_evidence_handle rejection; the current implementation can repair that narrow missing/null evidence-handle class using the child evidence manifest and retry parent synthesis once. The latest matrix did not exercise that repair path because every initial parent synthesis validated before repair, so the repair loop is framed as a tested runtime-control response rather than a benchmark win. The chunk explicitly limits generalization claims: it does not establish full baseline sweep, token usage, or cost-profile generalization across providers. The next subsection begins an operational memory experiment.

result.parent_summary: The supplied target context covers paper/main.tex lines 1-480 of a LaTeX article titled "RLM-FORGE: Runtime-Lifted Recursive Language Models for Agent Infrastructure" by JQ Lee, dated May 4, 2026. Across the six completed child chunks, the paper frames RLM-FORGE as a systems/runtime implementation of Recursive Language Model behavior rather than a new recursive model checkpoint. The abstract and introduction state that RLM can be runtime-lifted into an existing agent runtime: Hermes supplies bounded provider-swappable inner calls, Ouroboros owns recursive state, scheduling, trace replay, acceptance-criteria decomposition, and state mutation, and TraceGuard validates parent synthesis against accepted child evidence before claims can be committed. The background section defines RLM as an inference strategy in which an outer LLM call invokes itself or another LLM over transformed bounded input slices, describes Hermes as a provider-agnostic agent runtime with RPC tools and isolated subagents, and identifies Ouroboros components reused unchanged, including a typed acceptance-criteria tree with depth cap five and a HermesCliRuntime adapter. The system-design sections define runtime lifting as converting the RLM inference pattern into a stable runtime contract with bounded context selection, inner-LM invocation, evidence recording, and outer decisions to recurse, summarize, synthesize, retry, or terminate. They distinguish runtime-lifted RLM from ordinary map-reduce by requiring a traceable call graph, typed modes, evidence handles, and accepted or rejected child results. They also specify the ownership split: Hermes performs bounded local inference and must not recursively invoke Ouroboros or ooo commands, while Ouroboros remains the sole recursive controller and TraceGuard decides whether child-observed evidence supports parent claims. The lifecycle and integration sections describe host-side guardrail validation, context binding, trace events, Hermes invocation, JSON-envelope validation, accepted-result commits, invalid-response failure recording, and follow-up scheduling. Two integration paths are presented: a dedicated RLMOuterScaffoldLoop exercised by ooo rlm, and an optional Hermes-backed decompose_ac path that preserves default ooo run and ooo evolve behavior when hermes_runtime is absent. The experiment sections frame the work as a runtime/control-surface demonstration rather than a leaderboard-quality claim. A deterministic truncation fixture is described, with vanilla and recursive runs receiving the same selected-omitted partition and the live fixture reporting a tie. The environment table records Hermes CLI v0.11.0, a custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint, a provider-anonymized frontier-tier model, Ouroboros dependency commit 5b4bc10e, command ooo rlm --truncation-benchmark, fixture rlm-long-context-truncation-v1, sub-call counts vanilla=1 and recursive=5, and no persisted latency, token, or monetary-cost telemetry. The portability section reports that the primary RLM-FORGE+TraceGuard contract was executed across 8 fixtures and 3 runtime families for 24 live cells, holding fixtures, prompts, schemas, scoring, and TraceGuard logic constant while varying adapter and model alias. The reported primary matrix passes for hermes_glm/HermesCliRuntime/glm-4.7, claude_code_opus47/ClaudeAgentAdapter/opus, and codex_gpt55/CodexCliRuntime/gpt-5.5, each with 8/8 pass and 24 mandatory contract passes overall. The paper also describes a narrow missing_evidence_handle repair path motivated by an earlier Hermes+GLM failure, while explicitly limiting the claim because the latest matrix did not exercise that repair path and does not establish the full 96-cell baseline sweep, token usage, or cost-profile generalization across providers.

The runtime architecture separates responsibilities: Hermes performs bounded inner calls, Ouroboros owns recursive orchestration and state mutation, and TraceGuard validates parent synthesis against accepted child evidence.

The paper identifies preserved RLM runtime control surfaces: bounded context partitioning, explicit parent-child call graphs, replayable child evidence, inspectable intermediate claims, and rejectable parent synthesis before state mutation.
Hermes is constrained to local bounded inference and is explicitly prohibited from recursively invoking Ouroboros or ooo commands inside the RLM loop.

RLM-FORGE has two integration paths: a dedicated RLMOuterScaffoldLoop used by ooo rlm, and an optional Hermes-backed decompose_ac extension that leaves default ooo run and ooo evolve behavior unchanged when hermes_runtime is absent.
The experiment is framed as a runtime/control-surface validation, not a broad quality benchmark; the live truncation fixture reports a tie after scorer correction.
The primary portability run reports 24 mandatory contract passes across Hermes+GLM, Claude Code, and Codex under the same RLM-FORGE+TraceGuard contract.
Memory is treated as operational policy or schema/retry prior rather than admissible factual evidence; accepted evidence is rebuilt from fresh child outputs.
The repair loop is a bounded runtime-control response for missing or null evidence handles, not a benchmark win in the latest matrix because all initial parent syntheses validated before repair.

accepted_claims.text: RLM-FORGE's main claim is systems-level: it runtime-lifts RLM into existing agent infrastructure rather than introducing a new model architecture.

The runtime architecture separates responsibilities: Hermes performs bounded inner calls, Ouroboros owns recursive orchestration and state mutation, and TraceGuard validates parent synthesis against accepted child evidence.

The paper identifies preserved RLM runtime control surfaces: bounded context partitioning, explicit parent-child call graphs, replayable child evidence, inspectable intermediate claims, and rejectable parent synthesis before state mutation.
Hermes is constrained to local bounded inference and is explicitly prohibited from recursively invoking Ouroboros or ooo commands inside the RLM loop.

RLM-FORGE has two integration paths: a dedicated RLMOuterScaffoldLoop used by ooo rlm, and an optional Hermes-backed decompose_ac extension that leaves default ooo run and ooo evolve behavior unchanged when hermes_runtime is absent.
The experiment is framed as a runtime/control-surface validation, not a broad quality benchmark; the live truncation fixture reports a tie after scorer correction.
The primary portability run reports 24 mandatory contract passes across Hermes+GLM, Claude Code, and Codex under the same RLM-FORGE+TraceGuard contract.
Memory is treated as operational policy or schema/retry prior rather than admissible factual evidence; accepted evidence is rebuilt from fresh child outputs.
The repair loop is a bounded runtime-control response for missing or null evidence handles, not a benchmark win in the latest matrix because all initial parent syntheses validated before repair.

residual_gaps: Only paper/main.tex lines 1-480 were supplied; later sections, bibliography, appendices, code artifacts, trace logs, and any content after the beginning of the operational memory experiment are outside the supplied context.

Several empirical claims are reported in the supplied text, including truncation-fixture parity, deterministic ablations, memory-enabled matrix results, and a 24-cell primary portability pass, but the underlying raw traces, full benchmark data, scoring code, and artifact contents are not supplied here for independent verification.

The 96-cell matrix is described as a plan, while the supplied text states that only the primary 24 live cells are executed in this version; no conclusion should be drawn about the full baseline sweep.

The supplied text explicitly states that latency, token count, and monetary cost were not persisted for the live truncation artifact and that cost-profile generalization across providers is not established.

The operational memory experiment begins at line 479 but substantive details are outside the supplied context, so parent synthesis can only report the earlier abstract/introduction-level memory claims and the transition into that section.
